text: This video vignette shows tip and tricks to increase the surgical success rate of the Marcius approach for the repair of Crohn's-related recurrent low rectovaginal fistula. The procedure was performed under spinal anesthesia with the patient in the lithotomy position.

A vertical incision is made at the level of the vaginal introitus, allowing for careful dissection of the rectovaginal septum. The septum is circumferentially freed up to a certain distance from the fistula orifice to facilitate repair.
The surgeon repairs the fistula from the vaginal side using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures, following a curettage of the fistula orifice from the rectal side.

The surgeon makes a vertical left paravaginal incision, seven centimeters in length, to access the bulbocavernosus muscle. The muscle is carefully mobilized along with surrounding fibroadipose tissue, while preserving its posterolateral blood supply from the internal pudendal artery. The flap is prepared with adequate length before its superior intersection.
The surgeon transposes the isolated bulbocavernosus muscle into the prepared subcutaneous tunnel, ensuring the muscle's vascularized tissue provides protection for the direct repair.

The surgeon cuts the distal limb of the flap to prevent bulking of the muscle at the rectovaginal septum, which could cause dyspareunia. The graft is then fixed with interrupted two-zero absorbable sutures, and the vertical paravaginal incision is closed, with a pain rose drain left in place.

The postoperative period was uneventful and after a new evaluation under anesthesia confirming the healing of the rectovaginal fistula, the ostomy was closed three months after surgery. No recurrence nor any episode of dyspareunia occurred.
